relevant_segments.text: The Gromov-Wasserstein (GW) distance aims at matching distributions defined in different metric spaces. It is defined as follows:

Definition 2.1 (Gromov-Wasserstein). Suppose that we are given two unregistered complete separable metric spaces (X, dX), (Y, dY ) accompanied with Borel probability measures µ, ν respectively. The GW distance between µ and ν is defined as
where Π(µ, ν) is the set of all probability measures on X × Y with µ and ν as marginals.

relevant_segments.text: Definition 2.2 (Robust Gromov-Wasserstein). Suppose that we are given two unregistered complete separable metric spaces (X, dX), (Y, dY ) accompanied with Borel probability measures µ, ν respectively. The Robust GW between µ and ν is defined as
and (π1, π2) are two marginals of the joint distribution π, defined by π1(A) = π(A × Y ) for any Borel set A ⊂ X and π2(B) = π(X ×B) for any Borel set B ⊂ Y .

Theorem 2.3. Let µ and ν be two distributions corrupted by fractions ϵ1 and ϵ2 of outliers, respectively. Specifically, µ is defined as (1− ϵ1)µc + ϵ1µa, and ν is defined as (1− ϵ2)νc + ϵ2νa, where µc and νc represent the clean distributions, and µa and νa represent the outlier distributions. Then,

relevant_segments.text: To start with our algorithmic developments, we consider the discrete case for simplicity and practicality, where µ and ν are two empirical distributions, i.e., µ = ∑n i=1 µiδxi and ν = ∑m j=1 νjδyj . relevant_segments.section_heading: 3.2 Bregman Proximal Alternating Linearized Minimization (BPALM) Method
relevant_segments.text: As Problem (3) is non-convex and involves three variables, we employ BPALM [5, 2] to solve it. By choosing the KL divergence as Bregman distance, the updates of this algorithm are given by:

relevant_segments.section_heading: 3.2 Bregman Proximal Alternating Linearized Minimization (BPALM) Method
relevant_segments.text: Here, tk, ck, and rk are stepsizes in BPALM.
